panel.origin: Scoring three goals in a single match by one player — one of the game's signature individual achievements.
panel.mixology: He completed his hat-trick in the 88th minute, and the home crowd roared as the ball boy fished it out for him to keep as a souvenir.
panel.examples: Borrowed from cricket, where a bowler taking three wickets with consecutive balls earned a new hat. Soccer adopted the term for three goals, though they needn't be consecutive.

panel.origin: Moving the ball past opponents with a series of close touches while keeping it under control at the feet.
panel.mixology: He dribbled through three defenders in a phone-booth of space, the ball seemingly glued to his laces as bodies lunged and missed.
panel.examples: From the old English 'drib,' to let fall in drops or small movements — the same root as a basketball dribble, describing the ball moving in short hops.

panel.origin: A pass played into the open space behind the defense for a teammate to run onto, often springing a scoring chance.
panel.mixology: She threaded a perfect through-ball between the center-backs, and the striker was suddenly clean through on goal with only the keeper to beat.
panel.examples: Descriptive — the ball is played 'through' the defensive line. The term grew with the tactical vocabulary of the modern passing game.

panel.origin: An acrobatic shot where a player kicks the ball backward over their own head while airborne, body parallel to the ground.
panel.mixology: He met the cross with a bicycle kick, scissoring his legs in mid-air, and the ball flew into the top corner for goal of the season.
panel.examples: Named for the pedaling motion of the legs in the air. Known as 'chilena' in much of South America, where it was popularized in early-20th-century Chile.

panel.origin: Playing the ball through an opponent's legs and collecting it on the other side — one of the game's most humiliating skills.
panel.mixology: The winger nutmegged the defender with a casual flick, and the bench erupted; nothing wounds a defender's pride quite like a meg.
panel.examples: British slang of uncertain origin; one popular story ties it to 19th-century nutmeg traders' trickery, though the link to the football move is unproven.

panel.origin: Striking the ball cleanly out of the air before it bounces — a difficult, spectacular way to shoot or clear.
panel.mixology: The ball dropped over his shoulder and he volleyed it first-time, catching it so sweetly the net barely had time to ripple.
panel.examples: From 'volley' meaning a simultaneous discharge, via tennis, where it likewise means striking the ball before it bounces. Ultimately from French 'volée,' a flight.

panel.origin: A quick exchange of two passes between teammates to slip past a defender, also called a 'give-and-go' or 'wall pass.'
panel.mixology: They played a slick one-two on the edge of the box, the defender left flat-footed as the ball came straight back into the gap.
panel.examples: Named for its two-pass rhythm. The alternative 'wall pass' captures the idea of using a teammate like a wall to bounce the ball off.

panel.origin: A pass played from a wide area into the penalty box, aiming to set up a header or shot on goal.
panel.mixology: He whipped in a dangerous cross, and three attackers and two defenders all rose together — a moment of pure chaos in front of goal.
panel.examples: Descriptive — the ball is played 'across' the field from the flank toward the center, a staple of attacking play since the earliest days of the game.

panel.origin: Scoring two goals in a single match — one short of a hat-trick.
panel.mixology: Her brace turned the game on its head; two goals in five first-half minutes silenced a hostile away crowd.
panel.examples: From the hunting term 'brace,' meaning a pair (of birds or game). Football borrowed it as an elegant word for a player's two goals.

panel.origin: A coordinated defensive move where the back line steps forward in unison to leave an attacker offside as the ball is played.
panel.mixology: The back four sprang the offside trap perfectly, stepping up as one, and the striker's celebration died as the linesman's flag shot up.
panel.examples: Named for 'trapping' an attacker in an offside position. It became a defining tactic of organized defenses through the 20th century.

panel.origin: Aggressively closing down opponents to win the ball back high up the pitch rather than dropping deep to defend.
panel.mixology: Their high pressing suffocated the opposition, swarming every defender who touched the ball until a panicked pass was finally intercepted.
panel.examples: From 'to press,' to apply pressure. 'Gegenpressing' — counter-pressing immediately after losing the ball — entered the global vocabulary via German coaches in the 2010s.

panel.origin: A match in which a team concedes no goals — credited especially to the goalkeeper and defense.
panel.mixology: The keeper kept his fifth clean sheet in a row, and the defenders mobbed him; in their world, zero on the scoreboard is its own kind of goal.
panel.examples: From the idea of a scoresheet left 'clean' of any goals conceded — a metaphor for an unblemished defensive record.

panel.origin: A defensive action to dispossess an opponent of the ball using the feet, ranging from a clean poke to a sliding challenge.
panel.mixology: She timed the sliding tackle to perfection, taking ball cleanly and turning defense into attack in a single sweep of her leg.
panel.examples: From the general sense of 'to tackle,' meaning to take hold of or grapple with — shared with rugby and American football, though the soccer version forbids using the hands or body to bring a player down.

panel.origin: Closely tracking and guarding an opponent to prevent them from receiving the ball or creating chances.
panel.mixology: He was assigned to man-mark the playmaker and followed him everywhere, even to the corner flag, refusing to give him a single yard.
panel.examples: From 'to mark,' to keep watch on or note the position of. 'Zonal marking' versus 'man-marking' is one of defending's oldest tactical debates.

panel.origin: Kicking or heading the ball away from your own goal to safety, prioritizing distance over possession.
panel.mixology: Under siege in the box, the defender hooked a desperate clearance into the stands — ugly, but exactly what the moment demanded.
panel.examples: Descriptive — the defender 'clears' the danger from around the goal. A 'clearance off the line' is the dramatic last-ditch version.

panel.origin: A defensive setup where a team sits deep near its own goal in compact lines, inviting pressure to hit on the counter.
panel.mixology: Two goals up away from home, they dropped into a low block, ten men behind the ball daring the hosts to break them down.
panel.examples: Modern tactical terminology; the 'block' is the compact shape of defenders, and 'low' describes how deep toward their own goal they sit.

panel.origin: A kick awarded when the defending team puts the ball over its own goal line, taken from the corner arc — a prime scoring chance.
panel.mixology: The corner curled in toward the near post, and their towering center-back ghosted in to thump a header past the stranded keeper.
panel.examples: Descriptive — the kick is taken from the corner of the field. It has been part of the game since the laws were codified in the 19th century.

panel.origin: A kick awarded after a foul, taken from where the offense occurred — either a direct shot on goal or a pass to a teammate.
panel.mixology: From 25 yards he stood over the free-kick, took three steps, and bent it over the wall into the top corner — the keeper never moved.
panel.examples: Descriptive — the kick is taken 'free' of any opponent within the required distance. Direct and indirect free-kicks have been distinguished since the early laws.

panel.origin: A tiebreaker of alternating penalty kicks used to decide a drawn knockout match — football's most nerve-shredding finale.
panel.mixology: It came down to the fifth kick of the shootout; he placed the ball, exhaled, and the weight of a nation pressed down on a single swing of his boot.
panel.examples: Introduced in the 1970s to replace coin tosses and replays for deciding tied matches, and now the dramatic climax of major tournaments.

panel.origin: Returning the ball to play by throwing it with both hands from behind the head, after it crosses the sideline.
panel.mixology: His long throw-in was a weapon in itself, hurled into the box like a corner and causing the same panic among defenders.
panel.examples: One of the oldest restart laws, and notable as the only time an outfield player legally uses their hands — a quirk dating to the game's earliest rules.

panel.origin: A line of defending players standing shoulder to shoulder to block a free-kick's path to goal.
panel.mixology: The wall jumped as one, but he'd drilled the free-kick low underneath them, exploiting the gap their own leap had opened.
panel.examples: Named for the literal human 'wall' formed in front of the kicker. Goalkeepers organize its position to cover one side while they guard the other.

panel.origin: Any restart of play from a dead-ball situation — corner, free-kick, throw-in, or penalty — that teams rehearse as planned routines.
panel.mixology: They'd practiced the set-piece all week, a decoy run dragging a marker away before the real target arrived unmarked at the back post.
panel.examples: From the idea of a 'set' or staged, pre-arranged play, as opposed to open, flowing action. A large share of goals come from these rehearsed moments.

panel.origin: Video Assistant Referee — officials reviewing decisions on goals, penalties, red cards, and mistaken identity via replay.
panel.mixology: The goal stood, then didn't; after a long VAR check the offside lines appeared and the celebration curdled into furious disbelief.
panel.examples: Introduced to top-level football in the late 2010s to correct clear and obvious errors, and instantly one of the sport's most divisive innovations.

panel.origin: A card shown for a serious foul or second bookable offense, sending the player off and forcing their team to continue a man down.
panel.mixology: The reckless lunge earned a straight red, and his ten teammates traded glances — forty minutes to survive with a man fewer.
panel.examples: Part of the color-coded system introduced at the 1970 World Cup, reportedly inspired by traffic lights so decisions read clearly across language barriers.

panel.origin: A caution shown for a less serious offense; two in one match equal a red and an ejection.
panel.mixology: Already on a yellow, he pulled out of a challenge he'd normally fly into — one rash moment now and he'd leave his team short.
panel.examples: The companion to the red in the 1970 color-coded system; the idea came to referee Ken Aston while sitting at a traffic light.

panel.origin: A player is offside if they are nearer the opponents' goal line than both the ball and the second-last defender when the ball is played to them.
panel.mixology: He timed his run a half-second early and was caught offside by inches — the flag denying what would have been a stunning solo goal.
panel.examples: One of football's oldest and most debated laws, present since the 19th century to stop attackers from simply camping by the goal.

panel.origin: An offense in which a player other than the goalkeeper deliberately touches the ball with the hand or arm.
panel.mixology: The defender's arm was raised and the ball struck it; penalty given, and a long argument began over whether it was truly deliberate.
panel.examples: As old as the game's separation from rugby, when handling the ball became the defining forbidden act for outfield players. Its exact interpretation is endlessly contested.

panel.origin: Extra minutes added at the end of each half to make up for time lost to injuries, substitutions, and delays. Also 'injury time.'
panel.mixology: Deep into stoppage time, with the board showing six added minutes, he rose to head home the equalizer — the cruelest, sweetest of late goals.
panel.examples: Born from the referee's duty to account for 'time lost,' a power written into the laws so that wasted minutes don't simply vanish from the game.

panel.origin: When a referee lets play continue after a foul because stopping it would benefit the offending team — 'playing the advantage.'
panel.mixology: The referee waved play on, advantage, and seconds later the attack he'd allowed to continue ended in the back of the net.
panel.examples: From the principle that the fouled team should not be penalized twice; the referee judges whether stopping play would hand the offender an unfair reprieve.

panel.origin: An affectionate nickname for soccer itself, evoking its flow, artistry, and global appeal.
panel.mixology: On nights like this — a flowing team move finished with a flick — you understood exactly why they call it the beautiful game.
panel.examples: Popularized by Brazilian legend Pelé, who titled his autobiography with the phrase ('o jogo bonito'), though its use predates him in football writing.

panel.origin: Hardcore, organized supporter groups known for choreographed displays, banners, flares, and unwavering, often intense devotion.
panel.mixology: The ultras unfurled a banner covering the entire stand, a wall of color and smoke, their drums never pausing for ninety minutes straight.
panel.examples: From the Italian 'ultrà,' meaning extreme, the movement took root in 1960s-70s Italy and spread across the football world.

panel.origin: The fixture between Real Madrid and FC Barcelona — one of the fiercest and most-watched club rivalries on earth.
panel.mixology: El Clásico stops the country; for ninety minutes the political and sporting history of two cities is poured into a single football match.
panel.examples: Spanish for 'the classic,' the term marks the meeting of Spain's two giants, charged with regional, historical, and political rivalry far beyond sport.

panel.origin: A match between two local rival clubs from the same city or region, usually the most heated game on the calendar.
panel.mixology: The local derby divided families and workplaces for a week; in this city you were one or the other, and everyone knew which.
panel.examples: Often traced to Derby, England, and possibly the chaotic 'Shrovetide' folk-football games there — though the exact origin of the sporting sense is debated.

panel.origin: Winning three major trophies in a single season — typically the domestic league, domestic cup, and the top continental competition.
panel.mixology: They sealed the treble in June, an exhausted, jubilant squad lifting a third trophy that would etch the season into the club's history forever.
panel.examples: Descriptive, from 'treble' meaning threefold. The continental treble is one of the rarest and most celebrated achievements in the club game.

panel.origin: Playing ultra-defensively with nearly the whole team behind the ball, leaving almost no attacking intent.
panel.mixology: Clinging to a one-goal lead, they parked the bus completely, eleven men camped in their own box daring anyone to find a way through.
panel.examples: A vivid modern idiom — as if a bus were parked across the goal — popularized in English football in the 2000s to describe stifling, defense-only tactics.
